text:
Gail Devers芒聙聶s story is too remarkable to fit into a single introductory paragraph. The fastest version: elite Olympian comes down with unknown health condition, struggles at the 1988 Seoul Games, drops 79 pounds, nearly loses a foot; is diagnosed with Graves芒聙聶 disease, embarks on stunning comeback, competes in two more Olympics (five total), wins three gold medals; becomes the subject of a TV movie,Run For the Dream:The Gail Devers Story.

Sha芒聙聶Carri Richardson is favored to win the 100 meters Saturday. The last American woman to win the 100 meters at the Olympics? Gail Devers, at the 1996 Atlanta Games. SI spoke with Devers about the 100-meter dash, Team USA and what she芒聙聶s up to now, at 53.

(This interview has been edited for length and clarity.)

Gail Devers:Man, that芒聙聶s like 28 years ago. That was not my concern at all. I volunteer as a coach at a local high school. The kids are texting about all these things. Like,Gail,someone just broke your 24-year-old Olympic record[in the 100-meter hurdles]. I芒聙聶m like,I didn芒聙聶t know I had it,so OK! It芒聙聶s the same thing with bringing back the gold. I think this year, more than any other, we芒聙聶ve sent a great team to Paris. If this isthe year, it芒聙聶s going to be a joyous time.

GD:The U.S. team is one of the hardest teams to make. The best example is the 100-meter hurdles final [at U.S. trials] alone. Our fourth- and fifth-place runners [Nia Ali and Tonea Marshall, respectively, who both ran season-best times] could make the Olympic final. And they芒聙聶re sitting at home.

GD:It芒聙聶s hard to pick. Let芒聙聶s go back to trials and see who made the team. First of all, they芒聙聶re young. It芒聙聶s their first Olympic games. That makes you hungry. And the three that made the team [Richardson, Melissa Jefferson and Twanisha Terry] are from the same camp. They have the same coach [Dennis Mitchell]. Now, it芒聙聶s show-out time. If you watch Sha芒聙聶Carri, all of them, practice, they all show up. She芒聙聶s certainly capable. Now, it芒聙聶s forget about those five rings. It芒聙聶s the same thing we do every day, on the track芒聙聰execute what you芒聙聶ve been taught.

GD:Everything was different芒聙聰translating, transportation, hotels, training. But that芒聙聶s what progress is. You want each generation to get better and better. Now, we have the Diamond League. Back then, we didn芒聙聶t have meets in the states. So you had to go to Europe. We芒聙聶re all riding the bus together, riding on planes together, riding on the trains.

GD:That was when I should have been on top of the world. And I was, for a moment, because I had broken the American record for the 100-meter hurdles, in college. The statisticians are saying,If she just runs what she ran a couple months,we芒聙聶re gonna have a medal. And then I got to the Games and the bottom fell out. I ran slower than I ever ran. There was a problem. It took three years before I was ever diagnosed. I was constantly losing weight, tired all the time, couldn芒聙聶t sleep. I had problems with my eyes, bulging eye pain. As an athlete, you know your body, and you芒聙聶re like,Something芒聙聶s not right. Finally, I got diagnosed. Grave芒聙聶s disease is an auto-immune disease that causes hyper-thyroidism. Two years later, I came back, and I won my first Olympic gold medal. And then the other shoe dropped.

GD:I was still having problems, throughout that whole ordeal, with my eyes. Running over hurdles, everything was blurry. I could never see the hurdles clearly. I had learned to negotiate going over those hurdles. What the rhythm is. That there芒聙聶s eight steps from the blocks to the first hurdle. That there芒聙聶s three steps in between. Five steps off the last hurdle to the finish line. But I don芒聙聶t want to run in rhythm. Because if I芒聙聶m running in rhythm, I芒聙聶m running like everybody else. I want to sprint and take those hurdles. I thought it was the residual effect of the Grave芒聙聶s. But three decades later, I was having problems. I stopped driving at night. The lights coming at me were blinding. After three decades, I found out my eye problems were from a separate-but-related condition called Thyroid Eye Disease.I call him TED, because if somebody芒聙聶s been living with you for 30 years, he deserves a name. I had to become that voice, hope, advocate for other people.

GD:Take care of your body芒聙聰and I mean beyond getting ready for races. Go to doctors. Find a TED specialist. Think long-term. Go and find a specialist and ask for a comprehensive exam. 芒聙娄 Don芒聙聶t take the marathon route with healthcare. If you know better, do better. I hope my story gives people belief and hope. When [doctors considered amputating her foot], I芒聙聶m like,OK,you芒聙聶re going to take my foot.You芒聙聶re not going to take my joy.

GD:My dad always said I was a force to be reckoned with. So we have the Gale Force Foundation [also listed online as the Gail Devers Foundation, Inc.]. If I look back at my life, I芒聙聶m a teacher. Now, the world is my classroom.

GD:That芒聙聶s the plan.

Greg Bishop is a senior writer for Sports Illustrated who has covered every kind of sport and every major event across six continents for more than two decades. He previously worked for The Seattle Times and The New York Times. He is the co-author of two books: Jim Gray芒聙聶s memoir, 芒聙聹Talking to GOATs芒聙聺; and Laurent Duvernay Tardif芒聙聶s 芒聙聹Red Zone芒聙聺. Bishop has written for Showtime Sports, Prime Video and DAZN, and has been nominated for eight sports Emmys, winning two, both for production. He has completed more than a dozen documentary film projects, with a wide range of duties. Bishop, who graduated from the Newhouse School at Syracuse University, is based in Seattle.

title: Gail Devers Q&A: It's 'Show-Out Time' for Sha芒聙聶Carri Richardson, Team USA at Paris Olympics
description: The track legend was the last American woman to win the 100 meters at an Olympic Games. Will today's fastest woman on earth end the long drought?
